completeness.rationale: The triage captured most expected information: ~5,000 tasks, slow on description search vs fast on title search, v2.2→v2.3 regression, ~2 weeks ago, 10-15 second searches, and long descriptions. The only missing item from the expected list is the CPU spike during search, which the agent never asked about. Otherwise very thorough.

accuracy.rationale: The root cause hypothesis is remarkably close to the actual cause. The top hypothesis — that an FTS index was dropped or not migrated during the v2.3 upgrade, forcing a sequential scan — essentially matches the ground truth (v2.3 switched from FTS5 to naive LIKE queries, migration didn't preserve the FTS index). The ranked alternatives are also plausible and well-reasoned. All factual claims in the summary are consistent with the ground truth.

efficiency.rationale: Only 2 questions were asked, and both were essential and well-targeted. The first question efficiently combined timing/trigger and dataset size into one turn. The second question drilled into the specific search behavior that revealed the critical title-vs-description distinction. No wasted turns at all.

question_quality.rationale: Both questions showed genuine diagnostic reasoning. The first combined multiple high-value queries (timing, correlating events, dataset size) efficiently. The second question was particularly insightful — asking about search type differentiation (title vs description) directly surfaced the key diagnostic signal that pointed to the indexing issue. The reporter even noted 'Good question' and volunteered the long-description detail as a result.

actionability.rationale: The summary is exceptionally actionable. It includes clear reproduction steps, a ranked root cause hypothesis that's essentially correct, a concrete recommended fix (diff migrations, check FTS index, review query path), and even a proposed regression test. A developer could immediately start by diffing v2.2 and v2.3 migration scripts and search query logic.

notable_strengths: Extremely efficient — extracted nearly all key information in just 2 questions
Root cause hypothesis accurately identified the FTS index migration issue as the top candidate
The second question masterfully elicited the title-vs-description distinction, which is the critical diagnostic signal
Reproduction steps are concrete and actionable, including a comparison methodology between v2.2 and v2.3
Recommended fix is specific and directly addresses the actual root cause
notable_weaknesses: Did not ask about CPU/resource behavior during searches, missing the CPU spike observation
Did not capture OS/hardware details (Ubuntu 22.04, ThinkPad T14, 32GB RAM), though correctly noted these are less relevant given the version-correlated regression
Did not ask about or identify the specific database backend (SQLite), which would have further sharpened the hypothesis
most_insightful_question: Does the slowness happen on every search, or is it specific to certain types of queries? For example, is searching for a single common word just as slow as searching for an exact task title?
missed_opportunities: Asking whether CPU or memory usage spikes during the slow searches would have added supporting evidence for the full-table-scan hypothesis
Asking about the database backend (SQLite vs PostgreSQL) would have narrowed the root cause hypothesis further
